question_text: Study the comparison chart of Fayol and Taylor. Based on the diagram, which statement is CORRECT?
image_alt: Comparison table with 7 rows comparing Henri Fayol and F.W. Taylor across bases: Known as, Perspective, Focus, Applicability, Contribution, Background, and Unity of Command. Fayol column shows: Father of General Management, Top-level (Top-Down), Improving administration, Universal applicability, 14 Principles, French Mining Engineer, Strongly advocated. Taylor column shows: Father of Scientific Management, Shop-floor (Bottom-Up), Increasing productivity, Factory/industrial settings, Principles + Techniques, American Mechanical Engineer, Violated by Functional Foremanship.
explanation: The diagram clearly shows that Fayol's principles have universal applicability (all types of organisations) while Taylor's principles are primarily applicable to factory/industrial settings. This is one of the most fundamental differences — Fayol studied management broadly at the top level, while Taylor focused specifically on shop-floor efficiency in factories.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Both Fayol and Taylor focused on the same level of management
Taylor's principles are universal while Fayol's are limited to factories
Fayol's principles are universally applicable while Taylor's are primarily for industrial settings
Both gave the same number of principles

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram clearly shows Fayol = Top-level (Top-Down) and Taylor = Shop-floor (Bottom-Up). They focused on different levels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not reading the Perspective row of the comparison

elimination_hints.hint: This is reversed. The diagram shows Fayol = Universal and Taylor = Factory/industrial. Read the Applicability row carefully.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the applicability of the two thinkers

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Fayol gave 14 Principles while Taylor gave Principles + Techniques. Their contributions differ in number and nature.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not reading the Contribution row carefully

question_text: Study the organisational chart of Taylor's Functional Foremanship. According to the diagram, which foreman under 'Production Incharge' is responsible for ensuring that machines and tools are ready for workers?
image_alt: Organisational chart showing Factory Manager at top, branching into Planning Incharge (with Route Clerk, Instruction Card Clerk, Time & Cost Clerk, Disciplinarian) and Production Incharge (with Speed Boss, Gang Boss, Repair Boss, Inspector). All 8 foremen connect to Workers at the bottom. A warning note states: Violates Fayol's Unity of Command - worker reports to 8 bosses.
explanation: According to the diagram, under Production Incharge, the Gang Boss is responsible for keeping machines, materials, and tools ready for workers. The diagram shows each foreman's specific role: Speed Boss (timely work completion), Gang Boss (machines & tools ready), Repair Boss (machine maintenance), Inspector (quality check).
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Speed Boss's role as 'Timely work completion' — ensuring work is done on time, not preparing machines.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Speed Boss (pace) with Gang Boss (preparation)

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Repair Boss's role as 'Machine maintenance' — fixing broken machines, not preparing them for use.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Repair Boss (fixing) with Gang Boss (readying)

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Inspector's role as 'Quality check' — inspecting finished work, not machine preparation.

question_text: Study the diagram showing Taylor's techniques. Which technique determines the standard time for a task and which determines the rest intervals needed? Identify the correct pair from the diagram.
image_alt: Six cards showing Taylor's techniques: Time Study (determines standard TIME using stopwatch, sets fair day's work), Motion Study (studies body MOVEMENTS to eliminate wasteful ones, increases efficiency), Fatigue Study (determines REST INTERVALS needed during work, reduces exhaustion), Method Study (finds ONE BEST WAY to perform task, minimises cost), Standardisation (sets BENCHMARKS for process/quality/materials, ensures uniformity), Differential Piece Wage System (higher rate for efficient/lower rate for inefficient, motivates workers).
explanation: From the diagram: Time Study determines standard TIME for a task using a stopwatch (sets fair day's work), and Fatigue Study determines REST INTERVALS needed during work (reduces exhaustion). These are two distinct techniques — one measures duration, the other measures recovery needs.
topic: Taylor's Scientific Management: Principles and Techniques
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Time Study (standard time) and Fatigue Study (rest intervals)
Motion Study (standard time) and Method Study (rest intervals)
Method Study (standard time) and Time Study (rest intervals)
Standardisation (standard time) and Motion Study (rest intervals)

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Motion Study is about body MOVEMENTS (not time), and Method Study finds the ONE BEST WAY (not rest intervals).
elimination_hints.misconception: Misreading the diagram descriptions for Motion and Method Study

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Method Study finds the best METHOD, not standard time. And Time Study measures TIME, not rest intervals. This option swaps them.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the roles of Time Study and Method Study

elimination_hints.hint: The diagram shows Standardisation sets BENCHMARKS for quality/materials, not time. Motion Study is about movements, not rest.
